subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with a clear and appropriate purpose and focus throughout.
The research question is stated explicitly, focused and consistently connected to the discussion.
Section headings and overall structure map directly onto the declared focus, demonstrating deliberate planning.
weaknesses: One paragraph digresses into questionnaire subjectivity, causing minor drift from the core argument.
Methodological details are embedded rather than presented in a dedicated methodology section, weakening clarity of selection rationale.
Occasional repetition and lack of a concise roadmap in the introduction reduce overall precision.

However, occasional digressions, embedded methodological discussion instead of a clear methodology chapter, and minor repetitiveness place the work at the lower end of the 5â6 band, justifying a score of 5.

strengths: Specialist terminology (e.g., ARAS, GWAS, methylation) is used accurately and consistently throughout the essay.
Key theories and concepts are explained and applied coherently, integrating both seminal and recent literature.
Source application is consistently relevant, demonstrating an excellent grasp of disciplinary knowledge.
weaknesses: A minor acronym error (âFNDCâ instead of BDNF) indicates the need for tighter proofreading.

feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates excellent subject knowledge and consistently applies sources and terminology with precision. Minor lapsesâa single misâtyped acronym and occasional causal overreachâdo not substantially undermine the otherwise coherent and expert use of disciplinary content, warranting a topâband score of 6.

Analysis moves beyond description, quantitatively comparing twin correlations and interpreting EEG results via arousal theory.
Key limitations are identified (e.g., correlational designs, construct validity), and future research directions are occasionally proposed.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation is uneven: some methodological critiques (e.g., controls for EEG artefact, population stratification in GWAS) lack depth.

The conclusion summarizes findings effectively but stops short of a fully evaluative reflection and concrete future research plan.

While the evaluation is not consistently critical and some sections remain descriptive or tangential, the overall performance fits comfortably in the 7â9 band. An 8 reflects strong critical thinking tempered by identifiable areas for deeper evaluation.

strengths: The report follows IB conventions with a clear title page, table of contents, section headings, pagination, and figure captions.
The overall structure supports reader navigation and comprehension effectively.
weaknesses: Reference formatting is inconsistent (mixed styles, nonâalphabetical ordering) and there are minor typographical errors.

This level of organisation and polish warrants a midâband score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Throughout, the student employs specialist terminologyâARAS, GWAS, methylation, heritability coefficients, EEG alpha amplitudeâaccurately and in French, reflecting confident mastery of disciplinary language.  Concepts such as classical conditioning, epigenetic modification and neurophysiological arousal are defined and applied appropriately.

Minor lapses (e.g., the acronym error âFNDCâ for BDNF) and a handful of awkward phrasings prevent a perfect score, but overall usage is precise and consistent, supporting a band 5 rating within the 5â6 range.

Basic layout conventions are met: consistent pagination, clear headings and a legible table of contents.  However, reference formatting is inconsistent (mixed DOIs/URLs, duplicate entries, non-alphabetical ordering), and minor typographical errors (e.g., misaligned tables, varying line spacing) reduce overall polish.

Figures are minimally integratedâFigure 1 lacks in-text analytical referenceâand some French accents are inconsistent.  These weaknesses suggest that layout considerations are only partially met, corresponding to a score of 2 within the 1-2 band.

The essay demonstrates strong command of biological, genetic and psychological content.  Key theories (ARAS, Pavlovian inhibition, twin-study heritability, GWAS) are described accurately, and empirical findings are woven coherently into the argument.  The student integrates seminal (Moruzzi & Magoun, Fulker) and recent (Terracciano et al., 2010; Staes et al., 2022) literature, signalling mature engagement with sources.

A few inaccuracies persist (e.g., mis-typing BDNF as âFNDCâ) and causal claims occasionally overreach the data, but these do not substantially undermine the otherwise consistently relevant and well-applied knowledge base.  Consequently, the strand merits a score of 5 within the top band.

The student draws on a respectable variety of methodsâtwin studies, GWAS, neurophysiology, conditioning experiments and environmental investigationsâshowing breadth of methodological understanding.  Source choice is generally relevant and up-to-date, with both classic and contemporary papers.

Nonetheless, the rationale for selecting particular studies, databases or search strategies is only briefly noted, and there is limited critical reflection on why certain sources were prioritised or excluded.  Methodological description is uneven: some studies are outlined in great detail (e.g., Jones et al., 1981), while others receive cursory treatment.  This profile aligns with the 3â4 band, at the upper end because the range is appropriate and selection appears mostly informed, even if not fully justified.

The student states the domain, key theory and precise angle of inquiry in the opening lines and reiterates them throughout the essay.  The introduction clearly explains Eysenckâs model, defines extraversion and frames the biological-versus-environment debate, giving the reader an accurate sense of purpose.  The subsequent section headings (GÃ©nÃ©tique, MÃ©canisme cÃ©rÃ©bral, Facteurs environnementaux) map directly onto the declared focus, demonstrating deliberate planning and effective communication.

Minor lapses slightly reduce precision: one paragraph digresses into general problems of questionnaire subjectivity, and the conditioning subsection temporarily shifts away from the core biological issue.  Nevertheless, these detours do not seriously obscure the main line of argument.  Overall, topic communication is clear and appropriate, warranting placement in the 5â6 band, but some drift and occasional repetition keep it at the lower end of that band.

The investigation draws on a wide spectrum of credible primary and secondary sourcesâtwin and adoption studies, large-scale GWAS, neurophysiological experiments and environmental researchâdemonstrating good depth and currency.  Each body of evidence is generally linked back to the research question, illustrating clear relevance.

Although some supporting studies (e.g., chimpanzee epigenetics) are tangential to human personality, most research choices are well-aligned.  Occasional omissions (e.g., limited exploration of contradictory findings such as Grayâs critique) preclude the very highest band, yet the overall research base is solidly situated in the 7â9 range, meriting a score of 8.

The research question ("Dans quelle mesure le trait de personnalitÃ© d'extraversion a-t-elle une cause biologique ?") is expressed explicitly, is debatable and is narrow enough to be explored within 4 000 words.  Each substantive chapter links back to this question by weighing genetic, neurological and environmental evidence, showing sustained focus.

Some sections (e.g., the extended chimpanzee epigenetics discussion) edge toward evolutionary rather than strictly biological causation in humans, which marginally weakens the tightness of the focus.  However, the line of inquiry remains evident, and the essay consistently attempts to judge biological versus environmental contributions.  This solid coherence supports a high-band placement, though the partial drift places the work at 5 rather than 6.

The essay follows IB EE structural conventions: title page, word count, contents page, introduction, main body in clearly headed sections, conclusion and bibliography.  Pagination, figure captions and tables are present, reflecting purposeful organisation that supports understanding.

Nonetheless, some section transitions are abrupt, several sentences are repetitive, and the absence of an explicit methodology section or reflective conclusion on methods detracts from full structural effectiveness.  These issues place the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band, earning a score of 3.

The discussion progresses logically from genetics to brain mechanisms to environment and culminates in a synthesized conclusion favouring a gene-environment interplay.  The student identifies key limitations (correlational designs, construct validity of personality measures, generalisability from chimpanzees) and occasionally proposes future research directions.

However, critical evaluation is uneven: counter-evidence such as Grayâs objections or negative dizygotic correlations is acknowledged only briefly, and some methodological critiques (e.g., EEG artefacts) lack depth.  The argument is reasoned but not consistently critical, placing it at the upper end of the 4-6 band with a score of 6.

The essay offers coherent analyses that move beyond description: twin correlations are compared quantitatively, EEG evidence is interpreted via arousal theory, and environmental studies are weighed against genetic accounts.  The student frequently highlights methodological strengths and weaknesses, enriching interpretive depth.

Nonetheless, some analytical claims over-generalise correlational data as indicative of causation, and references to figures/tables are occasionally missing, limiting rigor.  These issues modulate effectiveness but the argument remains largely evidence-based and systematic, justifying a score of 7 within the 7-9 band.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear word count at the beginning, fulfilling IB formatting requirements and demonstrating attention to submission guidelines.
The inclusion of a detailed table of contents supports logical navigation and meets structural conventions effectively.
The introduction provides extensive theoretical background but lacks a concise preview of how each section will address the research question; a brief roadmap would improve focus and cohesion.
The explanation of Eysenckâs hierarchical model demonstrates accurate subject knowledge and effective application of specialist terminology to frame the biological basis of extraversion.
The research question is stated explicitly and focused, anchoring the investigation and ensuring clear guidance for subsequent analysis.
Figure 1 is well selected but lacks deeper analytical interpretation; the student should discuss how the ARAS diagram directly supports their argument on cortical arousal differences.

Methodological details are embedded within sections rather than in a clear methodology chapter; the student should include a dedicated methodology section outlining search strategies and selection rationale.
The acronym âFNDCâ appears to be a misâtyping of BDNF; consistent and accurate use of gene nomenclature is essential for credibility and precision.

The account of the Terracciano et al. GWAS omits key statistical details (e.g., pâvalues, confidence intervals); including these would strengthen transparency and allow better evaluation of effect sizes.
The study of Val66Met in Sardinia and Baltimore is not accompanied by discussion of population stratification controls; the student should address potential confounding due to ancestry differences.
The quotation of the APA definition is formatted inconsistently with citation style; ensure quotations and references adhere to one consistent academic style.
The detailed description of ARAS function demonstrates strong understanding of neurophysiological concepts and effectively integrates technical terminology.
Bibliography entries are inconsistently formatted and not alphabetized; ensure uniform citation style and correct ordering by author surname.
Several entries mix DOI and URL formats and omit italics for journal names; apply consistent reference formatting according to one citation style.
While EEG artefact sensitivity is noted, the student does not propose controls (e.g., filtering, artifact rejection); adding these would enhance the evaluation of internal validity.
The conditioning section is highly descriptive but offers limited critical linkage to the research question; it should more explicitly connect Pavlovian inhibition to extraversion causality.
The chimpanzee epigenetics study is interesting but tangential to human extraversion; this discussion weakens coherence with the central humanâfocused question.
The PCA analysis of epigenetic sites is summarized without explaining how components were retained; include criteria (e.g., eigenvalue thresholds) to clarify selection.
The use of peer ratings to infer introversion introduces ambiguity; recommending use of standardized inventories would improve construct validity.
The SES study does not control for intelligence or other mediators; addressing potential third variables would strengthen causal inference.
The conclusion effectively summarizes key points but stops short of critically reflecting on the research question and outlining concrete future research directions.
There is no reflective section on decisionâmaking or research planning; adding an analytical reflection would demonstrate engagement and metacognitive depth.
The continuation of the bibliography remains out of order and inconsistently styled; a final proofread of all reference details is needed.
The table of contents lists the bibliography starting on page 18, but this section spans to page 20; update page ranges to maintain accuracy.
Section headings are clear but transitions into new sections are abrupt; consider adding brief linking sentences to guide the reader smoothly.
